threats.title: forceInclusion Missing Time-Based Delay Check Allows Premature Message Inclusion
threats.hypothesis_description: The forceInclusion function only enforces a block-based delay check (l1BlockAndTime[0] + delayBlocks_ >= block.number) but completely omits the time-based delay check. The error ForceIncludeTimeTooSoon is defined in Error.sol but never referenced anywhere in the codebase. The delaySeconds state variable is stored via _setMaxTimeVariation and used in getTimeBounds for batch header creation, but is never checked during force inclusion. On any chain where block production rate varies or accelerates, the block-based threshold can be reached before the intended time-based delay expires, allowing premature force inclusion of delayed messages.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a demonstrable code defect: an error type is defined but never used, a configuration parameter is stored but never enforced in the relevant path. The dual delay design (blocks AND seconds) is clearly intended but only half-implemented in forceInclusion.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Only block-based delay check (l1BlockAndTime[0] + delayBlocks_ >= block.number) enforced; no corresponding timestamp check using delaySeconds or l1BlockAndTime[1]; ForceIncludeTimeTooSoon error defined in Error.sol line 136 but never used

preconditions: Chain block production rate is faster than expected or variable
delaySeconds configured to a meaningful value (non-zero)
Delayed messages exist in the bridge that haven't been sequenced

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: ForceIncludeTimeTooSoon error defined but unreferenced; delaySeconds stored but unused in forceInclusion path
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: No admin involvement needed; permissionless forceInclusion callable by anyone
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any period where block production rate exceeds 1 block per delaySeconds/delayBlocks seconds

operational_questions: What is the actual block time variance on target deployment chains?
What delaySeconds values are configured in production?
Can premature force inclusion disrupt sequencer batch ordering or L2 state?
validation_requirements: Verify ForceIncludeTimeTooSoon is never used in any code path
Measure block time variance on Ethereum mainnet and Arbitrum
Calculate maximum time-before-intended by which force inclusion can occur

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Monitor for periods of faster-than-normal block production
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Identify delayed messages in bridge that have not been sequenced
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Call forceInclusion when block threshold met but time threshold not yet met
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Messages are force-included earlier than the time-based delay intended
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: None - single transaction by any account

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Sequencer operations disrupted; messages force-included before intended time window; could affect L2 ordering assumptions
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Force inclusion timing guarantees, sequencer ordering window

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Basic - monitoring block times and calling forceInclusion
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: During periods of fast block production

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: ForceInclusion events when block.timestamp < expected time threshold
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Force inclusions during periods of accelerated block production
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Repeated force inclusions timed to exploit block time variance

threats.title: Staking Pool Deposit Theft After Assertion or Edge Stake Slashing
threats.hypothesis_description: AbsBoldStakingPool.depositBalance mapping is never decremented when tokens are transferred out of the pool to the rollup (via createAssertion) or challenge manager (via createEdge). If the pool's assertion/edge loses and the stake is slashed, the pool's token balance drops to zero but all depositBalance values remain unchanged. Any new deposits into the pool can be immediately withdrawn by existing depositors, effectively stealing new depositors' tokens. The pools use deterministic CREATE2 addresses with salt=0, making them discoverable and potentially still appearing valid to users even after the pool's purpose has been fulfilled and its stake lost.
threats.investigation_rationale: The accounting gap between token outflows (createAssertion/createEdge) and depositBalance tracking creates a theft vector. The pools explicitly document that excess tokens get stuck, but not the reverse case where deficit allows theft of new deposits.

proof_of_signal.pattern: depositBalance[msg.sender] += amount on deposit; withdrawFromPool checks amount <= depositBalance but never accounts for tokens transferred out via createAssertion/createEdge; no mechanism to reduce depositBalance when pool stake is slashed

preconditions: Pool has created an assertion/edge that subsequently lost (stake slashed)
Pool's ERC20 balance is below sum of all depositBalance values
New user deposits tokens into the exhausted pool
Old depositor still has non-zero depositBalance

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: depositBalance never reduced when tokens leave pool for rollup/challengeManager; createAssertion increases allowance and calls newStakeOnNewAssertion transferring tokens but not updating depositBalance
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent after pool's stake is slashed; pool contract persists at deterministic address

operational_questions: Can createAssertion/createEdge be called again on the same pool after a loss?
Are pool addresses still discoverable and appear valid after assertion/edge resolution?
What prevents new deposits into exhausted pools?
validation_requirements: Confirm depositBalance is not modified in any createAssertion/createEdge code path
Test scenario: deposit -> createAssertion -> assertion loses -> new deposit -> old depositor withdraws
Verify pool has no mechanism to mark itself as exhausted

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Deposit into a pool and participate in creating an assertion/edge
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: If the assertion/edge loses, stake is slashed but depositBalance persists
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Wait for or encourage new deposits into the same pool address
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Call withdrawFromPool to extract new deposits using old depositBalance credit
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: May need to attract new depositors to the exhausted pool

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Direct financial loss for new depositors; erosion of trust in staking pool mechanism
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Funds are irrecoverable once withdrawn by attacker
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All staking pool deposits and withdrawals

execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: Medium - requires a lost assertion/edge and new deposits
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: After assertion/edge resolution where pool's stake is lost

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Withdrawals from pools with zero or near-zero token balance
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Deposits into pools whose assertion/edge has already been resolved
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Old depositors withdrawing immediately after new deposits arrive

threats.title: Permissionless forceInclusion Manipulates Delay Buffer State for All Participants
threats.hypothesis_description: The forceInclusion function is callable by anyone and, when isDelayBufferable is true, calls buffer.update(l1BlockAndTime[0]) which modifies the global buffer state (prevBlockNumber, prevSequencedBlockNumber, bufferBlocks). This sets the buffer's reference points based on the force-included message's block number and the current block. A strategic caller can time forceInclusion calls to manipulate the buffer's depletion/replenishment trajectory, affecting the force inclusion delay for ALL subsequent messages. The buffer.update uses the LAST message's block number in the force-included range rather than the FIRST, which differs from the delay proof path.
threats.investigation_rationale: The buffer state is a shared resource that affects all participants. An unprivileged caller modifying it via forceInclusion creates a griefing vector where buffer manipulation affects sequencer operations and subsequent force inclusion deadlines.

proof_of_signal.pattern: buffer.update(l1BlockAndTime[0]) called in permissionless forceInclusion; updates global buffer state including prevBlockNumber and prevSequencedBlockNumber; affects delayBufferableBlocks calculation for all subsequent operations

preconditions: isDelayBufferable is true
Delayed messages exist that satisfy the force inclusion block delay
Buffer is not in its maximum state (room for state change)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Anyone can call forceInclusion to trigger buffer.update with a specific block number, resetting buffer reference points
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Whenever delayed messages pass the block-based delay threshold

operational_questions: Can strategic forceInclusion timing create cascading buffer depletion?
Does the asymmetry between forceInclusion (last message block) and delayProof (first message block) create exploitable differences?
What is the maximum buffer manipulation achievable through strategic forceInclusion?
validation_requirements: Simulate buffer state changes from strategic forceInclusion timing
Compare buffer trajectory with and without manipulation
Quantify impact on subsequent force inclusion deadlines

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Monitor delayed message queue for unsequenced messages
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Calculate optimal forceInclusion timing to maximize buffer depletion
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Execute forceInclusion to deplete buffer, shortening future force inclusion windows
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Subsequent force inclusions become easier, potentially cascading

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Sequencer's ordering window shortened; force inclusion deadlines reduced for all messages
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Buffer recovers over time via replenishment, but manipulation can be repeated
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Force inclusion deadlines, sequencer batch timing

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: When delayed messages are available and buffer is not synced

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: ForceInclusion events with specific timing patterns
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Buffer state changes not attributable to sequencer batching
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Repeated force inclusions by same address

threats.title: Zero replenishRateInBasis Creates Permanent Buffer Depletion
threats.hypothesis_description: DelayBuffer.isValidBufferConfig validates that replenishRateInBasis <= BASIS (10000) but does NOT enforce replenishRateInBasis > 0. If configured to zero, the buffer can never replenish once depleted. A single sequencer outage would permanently reduce the force inclusion delay to the threshold value, making the delay buffer mechanism a permanent one-way ratchet. The admin setter setBufferConfig also calls _setBufferConfig which uses the same validation.
threats.investigation_rationale: The buffer replenishment is the core recovery mechanism for the delay buffer. Allowing a zero rate creates a permanent degradation scenario that cannot be recovered without admin intervention (changing the config).
deployment_assumptions: replenishRateInBasis is set to 0 during initialization or via setBufferConfig
threats.config_evidence: DelayBuffer.sol isValidBufferConfig at line ~120 only checks config.replenishRateInBasis <= BASIS, not > 0

proof_of_signal.pattern: isValidBufferConfig checks config.replenishRateInBasis <= BASIS but not > 0; calcBuffer uses (elapsed * replenishRateInBasis) / BASIS which equals 0 when rate is 0

preconditions: isDelayBufferable is true
replenishRateInBasis is configured to 0
Any sequencer delay causes buffer depletion

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: isValidBufferConfig passes with replenishRateInBasis=0; calcBuffer replenishment term becomes 0
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Requires admin to set replenishRateInBasis=0 (or initialize with 0)
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent after any buffer depletion event

operational_questions: Is there any deployment validation that prevents zero replenish rate?
What are the deployed replenishRateInBasis values in production?
validation_requirements: Verify isValidBufferConfig accepts replenishRateInBasis=0
Test buffer behavior with rate=0 after depletion

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify deployment with replenishRateInBasis=0 (or wait for admin misconfiguration)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Cause or wait for any sequencer delay exceeding threshold
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Buffer depletes to threshold and never recovers
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Force inclusion delay permanently shortened to threshold blocks

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Permanent reduction of force inclusion delay; sequencer loses full delay window protection
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires admin intervention to change config; buffer starts recovering from depleted state
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Force inclusion timing, sequencer ordering window

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: None - passive exploitation of config error

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Buffer stuck at threshold value; no replenishment observed

threats.title: EdgeChallengeManager First Edge Stake Permanently Locked on Loss
threats.hypothesis_description: In EdgeChallengeManager.createLayerZeroEdge, the first edge created for a mutual ID has its stake held in the contract (receiver = address(this)), while subsequent rivals' stakes are immediately sent to excessStakeReceiver. If the first edge loses the challenge, its stake cannot be refunded (refundStake requires confirmed status). The stake remains permanently locked in the EdgeChallengeManager contract. Over many challenges, locked stakes accumulate, creating a growing pool of irrecoverable tokens.
threats.investigation_rationale: The asymmetric stake handling between first and subsequent edges means one stake per mutual ID can be permanently locked if the first edge loses. The refundStake mechanism only works for confirmed edges.

proof_of_signal.pattern: address receiver = edgeAdded.hasRival ? excessStakeReceiver : address(this); First edge stake kept in contract; refundStake requires edge.setRefunded() which checks confirmed status

preconditions: stakeToken != address(0) and stakeAmounts[level] != 0
First edge in a mutual ID group loses the challenge
No mechanism exists to recover losing first-edge stakes

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: First edge stake sent to address(this); losing edge cannot call refundStake; no sweep function for unrefundable stakes
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: None - occurs naturally when first edge loses
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent for each challenge where first edge loses

operational_questions: Does the EdgeChallengeManager library's setRefunded function check for confirmed status?
Is there any admin mechanism to recover stuck stakes?
How frequently does the first edge in a mutual ID group lose?
validation_requirements: Verify refundStake reverts for non-confirmed edges
Check if any upgrade or admin function can recover stuck tokens
Quantify potential token accumulation over protocol lifetime

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: A rival creates a competing edge (their stake goes to excessStakeReceiver)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: If the first edge loses, its stake is permanently locked
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Stake tokens accumulate in EdgeChallengeManager over time

threats.title: forceInclusion Uses Last Message Block Number Instead of First for Buffer Update
threats.hypothesis_description: In forceInclusion, buffer.update(l1BlockAndTime[0]) is called with the block number of the LAST delayed message in the force-included range. In contrast, delayProofImpl processes the FIRST new delayed message's block number for buffer updates. When force-including a range of messages (from old to new), using the last message's (more recent) block number results in LESS buffer depletion than if the first (oldest) message's block were used. This asymmetry means forceInclusion systematically underestimates the actual delay, maintaining a higher buffer than warranted and keeping the force inclusion window longer than it should be.
threats.investigation_rationale: The buffer depletion should reflect actual sequencer delay. Using the most recent message's block number instead of the oldest understates the delay, creating an inconsistency between the two buffer update paths.

proof_of_signal.pattern: forceInclusion calls buffer.update(l1BlockAndTime[0]) where l1BlockAndTime[0] is the LAST message's block; delayProofImpl calls buffer.update(delayProof.delayedMessage.blockNumber) for the FIRST new message

preconditions: isDelayBufferable is true
Multiple delayed messages accumulated over different blocks
Force inclusion covers a range of messages with varying block numbers

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Two different buffer update strategies for the same semantic operation (including delayed messages) depending on code path
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Whenever multiple delayed messages are force-included as a batch

operational_questions: What is the maximum discrepancy between first and last message block numbers in practice?
How does this asymmetry compound over multiple force inclusions?
Could this be intentional design to prevent buffer over-depletion during force inclusion?
validation_requirements: Compare buffer state after force inclusion vs equivalent delay-proof path
Quantify the buffer discrepancy for realistic message ranges
Verify whether the design intent documents explain this asymmetry

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Observe accumulation of unsequenced delayed messages spanning many blocks
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Force-include the range; buffer update uses most recent block, understating delay
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Buffer remains higher than warranted, keeping force inclusion delay longer
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Sequencer benefits from artificially maintained delay window

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Buffer doesn't accurately reflect actual sequencer delays; force inclusion deadlines may be longer than warranted
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires code fix to use first message's block number

threats.title: setBufferConfig Applies New Replenishment Rate Retroactively to Pending Period
threats.hypothesis_description: In _setBufferConfig, the new buffer config values (max, threshold, replenishRateInBasis) are written to storage BEFORE buffer.update(block.number) is called. The update's calcPendingBuffer uses the newly stored values to calculate buffer changes for the period since the last update (prevBlockNumber). This means the new replenishment rate is applied retroactively to the entire elapsed period. If the rate increases dramatically (e.g., from 100 to 10000 basis points), the buffer is over-replenished for the historical period. If decreased, under-replenished.
threats.investigation_rationale: Configuration changes should apply prospectively, not retroactively. Applying new rates to historical periods produces incorrect buffer values.

proof_of_signal.pattern: buffer.max, buffer.threshold, buffer.replenishRateInBasis updated before buffer.update(block.number); calcPendingBuffer reads self.replenishRateInBasis (now new value) for elapsed since self.prevBlockNumber

preconditions: isDelayBufferable is true
Admin changes buffer config (replenishRateInBasis specifically)
Significant time elapsed since last buffer update
bridge.delayedMessageCount() == totalDelayedMessagesRead (synced, triggering update)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Storage updated before buffer.update call; calcPendingBuffer uses newly stored values for historical period calculation
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Requires rollup owner to call setBufferConfig
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: During any buffer config change when system is synced

operational_questions: How much buffer error can result from typical config changes?
Could an admin exploit this to manipulate buffer state?
Is the buffer.update conditional (synced only) sufficient mitigation?
validation_requirements: Calculate buffer error for realistic config change scenarios
Test buffer state before and after config change with different rates

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Wait for significant time to elapse without buffer updates
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: buffer.update applies high rate retroactively, over-replenishing buffer

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Buffer value discontinuities after setBufferConfig calls

threats.title: BOLDUpgradeAction Caps Staker Refund at 50 Leaving Remaining Stakers Dependent on Patched Logic
threats.hypothesis_description: BOLDUpgradeAction.cleanupOldRollup iterates stakers with a hard cap of 50. Stakers at index 50+ are not refunded during the upgrade. They must manually withdraw via the IMPL_PATCHED_OLD_ROLLUP_USER implementation. Additionally, stakers currently in a challenge (currentChallenge != 0) are skipped even within the first 50. If IMPL_PATCHED_OLD_ROLLUP_USER doesn't properly handle all edge cases (challenged stakers, specific stake states), those stakers' funds could be permanently locked in the old rollup contract.
threats.investigation_rationale: The 50-staker cap is an arbitrary limit that could leave funds inaccessible if the patched implementation has any gaps in handling remaining stakers' withdrawal cases.
deployment_assumptions: Rollup has more than 50 stakers at upgrade time
IMPL_PATCHED_OLD_ROLLUP_USER correctly handles all staker withdrawal cases
threats.config_evidence: BOLDUpgradeAction.sol cleanupOldRollup at lines within perform function, cap at stakerCount > 50

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (stakerCount > 50) { stakerCount = 50; } - hard cap on staker iteration; stakers with currentChallenge != 0 also skipped

preconditions: Old rollup has more than 50 active stakers
BOLD upgrade is executed
Some stakers beyond index 50 have locked funds

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Hard cap of 50 in for-loop; remaining stakers depend entirely on IMPL_PATCHED_OLD_ROLLUP_USER correctness

operational_questions: How many stakers do production Arbitrum rollups typically have?
Does IMPL_PATCHED_OLD_ROLLUP_USER handle all staker states correctly?
Can challenged stakers withdraw after the old rollup is paused?
validation_requirements: Review IMPL_PATCHED_OLD_ROLLUP_USER implementation
Test withdrawal by staker at index 51+ after upgrade
Test withdrawal by challenged staker after upgrade

threats.title: RollupAdminLogic setBaseStake and setMinimumAssertionPeriod Allow Zero Values Disabling Economic Security
threats.hypothesis_description: RollupAdminLogic.setBaseStake has no minimum value check and can set baseStake to 0, eliminating the economic security requirement for assertion creation. Similarly, setMinimumAssertionPeriod can be set to 0, allowing assertions to be created with no time gap. Combined, these enable zero-cost assertion spam. In contrast, setConfirmPeriodBlocks correctly enforces > 0. The admin pathway relies on proxy routing for access control, so if governance is compromised, the protocol's economic security can be instantly disabled.
threats.investigation_rationale: Missing input validation on critical economic parameters could allow a compromised admin to instantly disable economic security. The asymmetric validation (setConfirmPeriodBlocks checks > 0, but setBaseStake and setMinimumAssertionPeriod do not) suggests incomplete parameter validation.

proof_of_signal.pattern: setBaseStake(uint256 newBaseStake) has no validation; setMinimumAssertionPeriod(uint256 newPeriod) has no validation; contrast with setConfirmPeriodBlocks which requires newConfirmPeriod > 0

preconditions: Admin (proxy admin / upgrade executor) calls setBaseStake(0) or setMinimumAssertionPeriod(0)
Compromised governance or malicious admin

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: setBaseStake(0) allows zero-cost assertion creation; setMinimumAssertionPeriod(0) allows unbounded assertion frequency

operational_questions: Is there governance-layer validation preventing zero baseStake?
What are the current production values for these parameters?
Does the user logic have additional checks on these values?
validation_requirements: Verify setBaseStake(0) succeeds without revert
Test assertion creation with baseStake=0
Test assertion creation rate with minimumAssertionPeriod=0

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Compromise admin key or governance mechanism
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Create assertions with zero stake at unlimited rate
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Flood the assertion tree, potentially confirming malicious state

threats.title: RollupAdminLogic _authorizeUpgrade and _authorizeSecondaryUpgrade Are Empty Allowing Unrestricted Proxy Upgrades
threats.hypothesis_description: RollupAdminLogic inherits DoubleLogicUUPSUpgradeable and overrides both _authorizeUpgrade and _authorizeSecondaryUpgrade with empty function bodies and no access checks. The code comments state 'this function doesn't revert as this primary logic contract is only reachable by the proxy's admin.' This creates a critical dependency: if the proxy admin routing is ever misconfigured, bypassed, or if the logic contract is accessed directly through a delegatecall from an unexpected context, unrestricted upgrades become possible. The security is entirely dependent on the proxy layer, not the implementation.
threats.investigation_rationale: Empty authorization functions represent a defense-in-depth violation. While the proxy admin routing is intended to provide access control, standard UUPS practice includes explicit checks in _authorizeUpgrade as a safety net.
deployment_assumptions: Proxy admin routing correctly restricts access to RollupAdminLogic functions
No alternative path exists to reach admin logic functions
threats.config_evidence: RollupAdminLogic.sol _authorizeUpgrade and _authorizeSecondaryUpgrade are empty; RollupProxy.sol uses AdminFallbackProxy for routing

proof_of_signal.pattern: function _authorizeUpgrade(address newImplementation) internal override {} function _authorizeSecondaryUpgrade(address newImplementation) internal override {} - both empty

preconditions: Proxy admin routing is misconfigured or bypassed
Alternative delegatecall path exists to reach admin logic
OR logic contract is called directly in a context where state matters

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Empty authorization functions; all security delegated to proxy routing; no defense-in-depth

operational_questions: Is the AdminFallbackProxy implementation provably correct in all scenarios?
Could storage collision in a proxy upgrade expose admin functions?
Are there any delegatecall chains that could reach admin logic unexpectedly?
validation_requirements: Audit AdminFallbackProxy routing logic exhaustively
Verify no delegatecall chain bypasses admin routing
Test that non-admin callers cannot reach admin functions

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify any misconfiguration or bypass in proxy admin routing
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Call upgradeTo or upgradeSecondaryTo through the bypass

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Total - malicious implementation can do anything
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Extremely high - may require social consensus fork

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Implementation change events from non-admin addresses
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Proxy routing returning admin logic to non-admin callers
